retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Several challenges exist regarding the application of hydroxyapatite in medicine. For instance, using hydroxyapatite as an implant presents inherent defects and fine porosity that can act as crack initiators. Following crack initiation, propagation can lead to catastrophic failure during use. Additionally, the application of bulk hydroxyapatite may result in a modulus mismatch between the bone and the implant, leading to disproportionate load sharing. [10] Conversely, hydroxyapatite, irrespective of its source, always contains trace elements such as fluoride ions (F - ) and hydroxyl ions (OH - ), which contribute to increased crystallite size and decreased solubility, thereby enhancing the strength of the apatite. [19] At the same time, elements such as phosphide ions (PO 3 3- ) and chloride ions (Cl - ) have been shown to weaken the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite by reducing crystallite size and increasing solubility. [10] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Several methods exist for producing hydroxyapatite from synthetic or natural sources. Synthetic hydroxyapatite is derived from raw materials such as calcium carbonate, calcium hydroxide, calcium nitrate, diammonium hydrogen phosphate, and ammonium hydroxide. The fabrication process of hydroxyapatite involves both wet methods and solid-state reactions, followed by calcination or sintering. These methods utilize chemical reactions that adjust the content of CaO and TCP to achieve the stoichiometric conditions necessary for hydroxyapatite formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Cellular functions : In the cells, phosphate is an important component of the lipid bilayer of cell membranes, DNA, RNA, and proteins. It is responsible for several enzymatic reactions within the cells, e.g., glycolysis and ammoniagenesis. It modifies the actions of different molecules by getting attached or detached from them in response to kinases and phosphates. It also carries out oxidative phosphorylation, which is the reaction that converts adenosine diphosphate to adenosine triphosphate, the energy currency of the cell. Phosphate has a role in the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin by regulating the synthesis of 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Clinical Significance -- Oxidative Phosphorylation Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Sources of CO are paint strippers, house fires, wood-burning stoves, automobile exhaust, and other gasoline- or propane-fueled equipment. A CO saturation monitor can detect CO levels. Ratios of carboxyhemoglobin to hemoglobin greater than 10% are likely to show as symptomatic. Regular pulse oximetry devices read the percent of bound hemoglobin, irrespective of what is bound. Therefore, when CO is bound rather than O2, a patient's pulse Ox may still appear normal and cannot be used reliably. Instead, a co-oximeter should be used. Treatment for CO poisoning is to dissociate the bound CO with O2. Providing 100% supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather or administering hyperbaric oxygen are options. [29] [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyapatite from natural sources is commonly fabricated from materials such as fishbone, coral, bovine bone, eggshell, and seashells through a calcination process. Hydroxyapatite produced from these natural sources is often nonstoichiometric due to the presence of trace ions. [13] [17] These traces of ions, which include cations such as Na + , K + , Mg 2+ , Sr 2+ , Zn 2+ , and Al 3+ , as well as anions such as F - , Cl - , SO 4 2- , and CO 3 2- , are beneficial for promoting rapid bone regeneration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: ATP is an excellent energy storage molecule to use as "currency" due to the phosphate groups that link through phosphodiester bonds. These bonds are high energy because of the associated electronegative charges exerting a repelling force between the phosphate groups. A significant quantity of energy remains stored within the phosphate-phosphate bonds. Through metabolic processes, ATP becomes hydrolyzed into ADP, or further to AMP, and free inorganic phosphate groups. The process of ATP hydrolysis to ADP is energetically favorable, yielding Gibbs-free energy of -7.3 cal/mol. [1] ATP must continuously undergo replenishment to fuel the ever-working cell. The routine intracellular concentration of ATP is 1 to 10 uM. [2] Many feedback mechanisms are in place to ensure the maintenance of a consistent ATP level in the cell. The enhancement or inhibition of ATP synthase is a common regulatory mechanism. For example, ATP inhibits phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK1) and pyruvate kinase, two key enzymes in glycolysis, effectively acting as a negative feedback loop to inhibit glucose breakdown when there is sufficient cellular ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Carbon Disulfide Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite regulatory efforts, hazardous incidents involving CS 2 continue to pose significant health risks due to the potential for large-scale exposure to this substance. Several international events have been reported in which industrial accidents led to severe health complications. In Korea, between the late 1980s and early 1990s, numerous workers at a rayon plant were exposed to high levels of CS 2 . This subsequently led to extensive epidemiological studies of individuals exposed to higher amounts of CS 2 over time. [3] In 1994, a major leak at a viscose rayon plant in India exposed both workers and nearby residents to dangerous concentrations of CS 2 . [1] Similarly, in 1996, a railroad tank car accident in Tennessee released a large amount of CS 2 into the surrounding community, resulting in the evacuation of approximately 500 people. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Phosphate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Of the total phosphate in the body, 85% is n the bones and teeth,1% in the extracellular fluid and the remaining 14% is distributed in other tissues where it is an important constituent of cell membranes, nucleic acids, high energy phosphate esters (ATP) and intracellular signaling proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Electric Potential and Capacitance -- Issues of Concern -- From Theory to Application
retrieved_docs.content: Electric potential and capacitance have many applications in power generation and energy storage. Every electrical appliance relies on the charge, electric potential, and capacitance function effectively. In a study by Roy et al, aspects of electric potential and capacitance were investigated concerning photogenerated electrical energy to enhance energy storage devices. In this work, Roy et al studied the capacitance of storage cells because capacitors are temporary batteries that hold a charge. However, capacitance is just one aspect of circuitry required to create effective electrical devices. Other aspects, such as current and resistance, are also integral for continuous electrical circuitry. Understanding a system's electric potential can create novel materials. Electrochemical engineering heavily relies on the accuracy of electric potential in fuel cells and batteries to maintain power distribution. By harnessing electric potential, for example, polymerization leads to discoveries such as bone regeneration or polymer interfaces to manufacture novel devices. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wet method produces nonstoichiometric hydroxyapatite powder that may contain impurities such as hydrogen phosphate, carbonate, chloride, and sodium ions. These impurities can lead to the formation of calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite. [1] Previous studies have identified these impurities as uncontrollable variables that can significantly alter the crystallographic arrangement and chemical properties of hydroxyapatite, ultimately affecting its dissolution process.

retrieved_docs.title: Acid Phosphatase -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The cost of sipuleucel-T is approximately $100,000 per month, making it one of the most expensive cancer treatments on the market. Although sipuleucel-T has shown superior efficacy to docetaxel, the cost difference may barely compensate for the added median life; therefore, funding becomes an obstacle. Fortunately, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid cover sipuleucel-T treatments. Nonetheless, involving financial personnel and social workers in the treatment process may facilitate funding. In addition, exploring equally effective yet less expensive options is certainly warranted to decrease the economic burden on society. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1430
retrieved_docs.content: Inorganic carbon is identified as the parent of the radioactive isotope Carbon‑14, and Carbon‑14 in turn serves as the parent of carbon and/or carbon compound‑containing products. This hierarchical relationship indicates that inorganic carbon gives rise to Carbon‑14, which subsequently provides the source material for products that contain carbon or carbon compounds, a concept that underlies radiocarbon tracing and the origin of carbon‑based substances in medical and scientific contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon and/or carbon compound‑containing product is linked to the radioactive isotope Carbon‑14 through a parent relationship, indicating that Carbon‑14 serves as the parent nuclide for products that contain carbon or carbon compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Inorganic carbon compounds are positioned within a hierarchical chemical classification in which carbon dioxide serves as their immediate parent, indicating that these compounds are derived from or closely related to carbon dioxide. Carbon dioxide itself is placed under the broader category of oxygen compounds, making oxygen compounds the ultimate parent in this hierarchy. Consequently, the relationship chain progresses from oxygen compounds to carbon dioxide and then to inorganic carbon compounds, reflecting a structured taxonomy of these chemical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium phosphate functions as an industrial aid, meaning it is employed in manufacturing and processing contexts to support or improve industrial operations. The concept of industrial aid therefore includes substances such as calcium phosphate that assist in various industrial processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 423
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base markers and blood gases are clinical parameters that reflect the status of key gaseous molecules in the circulatory system—carbon monoxide (CO), oxygen (O₂), and carbon dioxide (CO₂). CO, oxygen, and CO₂ belong to the broader families of oxides, gases, and oxygen‑containing compounds; CO and CO₂ are also classified as carbon‑containing products and inorganic carbon compounds, linking them to the radioactive isotope carbon‑14. CO functions as a gasotransmitter, an inverse subclass of signaling molecules, and is the causative agent in carbon monoxide poisoning. O₂ is the therapeutic agent used to treat carbon monoxide poisoning, highlighting the clinical interplay among these gases. CO₂ is specifically identified as an oxygen compound, reinforcing its classification within the larger group of oxygen compounds. This integrated view underscores the physiological, toxicological, and diagnostic relevance of carbon‑based gases, their inorganic forms, and related biomarkers in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: effects at the molecular level.” Calcium carbonate and aluminum hydroxide are specific antacid constituents; calcium carbonate is additionally classified as a mineral and as a structural component of mother‑of‑pearl. Carbon‑14, a carbon‑containing product, derives from inorganic carbon, which originates from carbon dioxide. This network of classifications and hierarchical relationships illustrates how antimetabolites, gasotransmitters, antacida, calcium carbonate, chlortetracycline, iron saccharate and related carbon‑based compounds intersect chemically and pharmacologically, informing their roles in diagnosis, therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Inorganic carbon is a chemical category that includes carbon monoxide (oxid uhelnatý) as a representative member, indicating that carbon monoxide is a specific form of inorganic carbon. Moreover, carbon monoxide is classified as a gasotransmitter, meaning it functions as a gaseous signaling molecule in physiological processes. Consequently, inorganic carbon, oxid uhelnatý, and gasotransmitery are interconnected, with carbon monoxide linking the broader class of inorganic carbon compounds to the functional group of gasotransmitters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2527
retrieved_docs.content: Isopropamide iodide is categorized as a bulk compounding chemical, and bulk compounding chemicals are classified within the broader chemical group of tricalcium phosphate, linking the specific compound to its larger chemical family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid uhelnatý (carbon monoxide) is a central molecule that serves as the parent entity for the class anorganické sloučeniny uhlíku (inorganic carbon compounds), indicating that these compounds are derived from or related to carbon monoxide. At the same time, oxid uhelnatý is classified as an antimetabolite, reflecting its capacity to interfere with normal metabolic processes, and it is also recognized as a gasotransmitter, a gaseous signaling molecule that modulates physiological functions. By linking the categories of inorganic carbon chemistry, metabolic inhibition, and gaseous cellular communication, oxid uhelnatý illustrates a multifaceted role that is relevant for both biochemical understanding and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric hydroxide and calcium phosphate are both categorized as types of industrial aid, indicating that they are substances used to support and facilitate various industrial processes. As forms of industrial aid, ferric hydroxide and calcium phosphate serve functional roles such as acting as additives, precipitants, or raw materials that enhance manufacturing, treatment, or production operations. Their classification under the broader concept of industrial aid underscores their importance in improving efficiency and outcomes in industrial settings.

retrieved_docs.content: Chlorbutanol and calcium phosphate are both classified as industrial aids, meaning each serves as a chemical agent used to facilitate various industrial processes. As types of industrial aid, these substances are employed to improve efficiency, act as catalysts, or modify material properties in manufacturing contexts, highlighting their functional role in industry rather than being primary end‑products.

retrieved_docs.content: Calcium citrate is a chemical compound that is derived from citric acid, with citric acid serving as its parent molecule.
